title: How To Track Qubits Through Space and Time (Or: Sailing in a Quantum Boat)
authors: James Bartusek, Zikuan Huang, Leo Orshansky, Henry Yuen
abstract: While quantum position verification aims to certify a prover's location using quantum information, existing security definitions only guarantee that part of the successful adversarial party is in the claimed location. This leaves open the possibility that a distributed team of adversaries can jointly simulate a prover in a way that defeats the intended meaning of ``being at a location'' in position-based cryptography.   We introduce stronger notions of position verification that we call quantum localization, which requires that there is a specified, unclonable state at the verified spacetime point -- and that this state can be found nowhere else. We show that quantum localization leads naturally to a meaningful notion of trajectory verification, in which quantum information is verifiably tracked through space and time. We construct quantum localization and trajectory verification protocols using quantum anchor states, which generalize coset states from unclonable cryptography. The security of our schemes is proven in the classical oracle (i.e. ideal obfuscation) model, which can be heuristically instantiated in the plain model using post-quantum indistinguishability obfuscation.   We also introduce and instantiate the concept of functionality localization, which guarantees that the adversary has the ability to compute a secret function at the verified spacetime point, and this function cannot be computed anywhere else. This raises the intriguing possibility of localizing computational capabilities in space and time.   More broadly, we believe our notions of quantum localization and our feasibility results provide stronger foundations for position-based cryptography.
summary: This paper advances quantum cryptography by proposing 'quantum localization' and 'trajectory verification' to overcome security flaws in existing position verification schemes. It constructs protocols using quantum anchor states to verifiably track quantum information through space and time. This work establishes stronger theoretical foundations for position-based cryptography by localizing both quantum states and computational functions.
main_problem: Existing quantum position verification security definitions are insufficient because they fail to prevent distributed adversarial simulation across space and time.
main_result: The authors introduce and construct protocols for 'quantum localization' and 'trajectory verification,' providing stronger foundations for position-based cryptography.
method: The work develops protocols using 'quantum anchor states' and generalizes concepts from unclonable cryptography to track quantum information through spacetime.
model_or_system: The framework relies on quantum information theory, specifically utilizing bipartite entangled states and the concept of tracking quantum states or computations across a specified spacetime trajectory.
key_observables: Fidelity of the joint state between anchor and vessel registers, and the success probability ($\eta$) of the extraction process.
important_parameters: The security proofs are established in the classical oracle (ideal obfuscation) model, which is heuristically instantiated using post-quantum indistinguishability obfuscation.
main_assumptions: The security proofs rely on the existence of one-way functions and the ideal obfuscation model.
figures_summary: Figure 2 illustrates a non-localizability attack on the f-BB84 protocol involving three provers, demonstrating how entanglement can be nonlocally distributed.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the weakness in existing QPV to introducing stronger notions (quantum, state, functionality localization). It then constructs protocols for trajectory verification, details the mathematical machinery using quantum anchor states, and concludes by addressing public verifiability.
why_interesting: While highly abstract, the concepts of localizing quantum information and computational capabilities in space and time provide novel theoretical tools for understanding the physical constraints on quantum information processing and security.
